title: practical english usage - Part 58
content:
(           We were poured water on.) They poured water on us. -> Water was poured on us.

He was stolen a bicycle from.) They stole a bicycle from him. -> A bicycle was stolen from him.

(          He was thrown stones at) They threw stones at him. -> Stones were thrown at him.

cannot become a passive subject.

If there is already a direct object, the second object (after the preposition) 2 Stones        were      thrown        at   him.

grammar *64  He is believed to be...

The hostages are expected to be released today.

I was told to be waiting outside the station at six o'clock.

He is believed to have crossed the frontier last night.

A  passive verb can be followed by a perfect, progressive or passive infinitive.

5  perfect,     progressive         and   passive      infinitives There was said to be disagreement between Ministers.

(= It is thought that there are ...) There are thought to be more than 3,000 different languages in the world.

structure is possible with there as a 'preparatory subject'.

With some verbs (e.g. say, think, feel, report, presume, understand), the passive 4 preparatory         there He was helped to get out of the country.

- They helped him  (to) get out of the country.

He was made to tell them everything.

- They made him  tell them everything.

He was seen to come out of the house.

- I saw him  come out of the house.

to (►  91). In passive structures to-infinitives are used. Compare: These verbs can be followed, in active structures, by object + infinitive without 3 hear,     see,    make      and   help but not (          They say his cotHt/any-t^lfe-in trouble.) His company is said to be in trouble.

Note that with say, the infinitive structure is only possible in the passive.

It is considered to be the finest cathedral in Scotland.

She is known to have been married before.

Moriarty is thought to be in Switzerland.

He is believed to be dangerous.

often occurs in news reports.

However, the passive structure (e.g. He is believed to be . ..) is common, and They believe him  to be dangerous, (more usual: They believe that he...) rather formal and often unusual.

With verbs like think, feel, believe, know, etc, the object + infinitive structure is 2  He  is  believed         to   be   dangerous.

They are allowed to visit Harry once a week.

We were told not to come back.

Felicity was chosen to be the Carnival Queen.

I was asked to send a stamped addressed envelope.

In most cases, these structures can be made passive.

We chose Felicity to be the Carnival Queen.

He asked me to send a stamped addressed envelope.

Many verbs can be followed by object + infinitive (►  98).

1  I was     asked       to   send     ...

64 He  is  believed         to   be   ...

grammar •  65  He was considered a genius.

My suitcase is packed. (= ... has been packed.) I think your ankle is broken. (= ... has been broken.) I got caught in the rain and my suit’s ruined. (= ... has been ruined.) all been cut up ...) The vegetables are all cut up - what shall I do now? (= The  vegetables have perfect passive.

Because of this, be + past participle can have a similar meaning to a present being shut - the result of the action) When I got there I found that the theatre was closed, (refers to the state of action of closing) The theatre was closed by the police on the orders of the mayor, (refers to the result (rather like adjectives). Compare: can have two meanings. They  can refer to the action, or they can describe the The  past participles of finished-result verbs, and some of their passive tenses, build, pack, close. Other verbs do not: for example push, live, speak, hit, carry.

Some verbs refer to actions that produce a finished result. Examples are cut, 66 My   s uitcase        is  pa   cked.    __________________ For more about object complements, ►     10.

The house has been made beautiful.

She was called stupid by the other children.

He was seen as a sort of clown.

Kathy was regarded as an expert.

Mrs Sanderson was elected President.

He was considered a genius by Queen Victoria.

In passive clauses these are 'subject complements’ ; they come  after the verb.
